disease.name: Inguinal Hernia
disease.note: The most common hernia — protrusion of abdominal contents through the inguinal canal. Bimodal distribution: congenital (indirect, via patent processus vaginalis in infants) and acquired (direct, through weakened posterior wall in adults). In Kenya, it is extremely common in men performing heavy labour (farming, construction). The lifetime risk of strangulation is 3-30%, highest in infants and femoral hernias. All inguinal hernias in children should be repaired electively to prevent strangulation — do not wait.
pathophysiologyDetail: Inguinal hernia occurs through the inguinal canal, an oblique passage through the abdominal wall that transmits the spermatic cord in males and the round ligament in females — the canal is a natural point of weakness because it breaches all three layers of the abdominal wall

Indirect inguinal hernias (60% of all inguinal hernias) result from a patent processus vaginalis — the peritoneal diverticulum that normally obliterates after testicular descent fails to close, creating a pre-formed channel for abdominal contents to follow the spermatic cord through the deep inguinal ring

In infants with indirect hernias, the processus vaginalis is patent in 80% of premature and 5% of full-term males; the high intra-abdominal pressure during crying forces bowel or omentum into this channel, producing a hernia that follows the cord into the scrotum

Direct inguinal hernias (40%) are acquired, resulting from weakening and attenuation of the transversalis fascia in Hesselbach's triangle (bounded by the inferior epigastric artery medially, the rectus sheath laterally, and the inguinal ligament inferiorly)

The transversalis fascia is the primary posterior wall of the inguinal canal — in direct hernias, this fascia bulges medially due to chronic intra-abdominal pressure from heavy lifting, chronic cough, constipation, or prostatic obstruction

In Kenya, the high prevalence of indirect inguinal hernias in young men (20-40 years) is strongly linked to occupations involving heavy lifting (farming, construction, boda-boda riding) and the chronic Valsalva associated with these activities

The 'shutter mechanism' of the inguinal canal — the contraction of the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles that normally narrows the canal during straining — becomes ineffective when these muscles are weak or when the canal is chronically dilated

Once the hernia sac enters the inguinal canal, it follows the path of least resistance — along the spermatic cord, through the external ring, and into the scrotum (indirect) — or pushes directly through the posterior wall (direct)

Incarceration occurs when the hernia contents become trapped at the external ring (indirect) or the defect edge (direct), with edema of the contents further compromising the ability to reduce — the narrowest point of the canal is the external ring

Strangulation in inguinal hernias is less common than in femoral hernias (5-10% vs 40%) because the inguinal canal is more elastic and can stretch, but once the external ring becomes a tight constriction band, the same cascade of venous congestion → arterial compromise → gangrene ensues

The distinction between indirect and direct hernias is anatomically important but does not change the management: both are repaired with mesh reinforcement of the posterior wall (Lichtenstein) or by plugging the defect (mesh plug for indirect)

In women, the round ligament within the inguinal canal is less robust than the spermatic cord, making the posterior wall relatively weaker, but the overall incidence is lower (1:7 female-to-male) because the processus vaginalis obliterates more completely in females

The molecular basis of inguinal hernia involves systemic connective tissue abnormalities: the transversalis fascia in hernia patients shows reduced Type I collagen content and a decreased Type I:III collagen ratio, producing mechanically weaker fascia that fails under repetitive stress

Matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, MMP-2, MMP-9) are overexpressed in the transversalis fascia of patients with direct inguinal hernias compared to controls, indicating an active proteolytic degradation of the extracellular matrix that weakens the posterior wall over time

Tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMP-1, TIMP-2) are correspondingly downregulated in hernia patients, shifting the MMP/TIMP balance toward matrix degradation — this imbalance correlates with the size and direct/indirect type of hernia

TGF-β1 expression is reduced in fibroblasts cultured from hernia patients, impairing the normal wound healing response to micro-injuries of the fascia — each episode of increased intra-abdominal pressure causes micro-tears that fail to heal properly, progressively weakening the transversalis fascia

Elastin fibre fragmentation and disorganization in the transversalis fascia has been demonstrated on electron microscopy, reducing the elastic compliance of the posterior wall and making it more susceptible to permanent deformation under repetitive loading

Smoking induces a systemic pro-inflammatory state that upregulates MMPs and downregulates TIMPs — this explains the 2-3 fold increased risk of inguinal hernia in smokers, and the even higher risk of recurrence after repair

A genetic component is supported by: (1) family clustering of inguinal hernias (2-3 fold increased risk in first-degree relatives), (2) association with COL1A1, COL3A1, MMP-2, and TGFB1 polymorphisms, and (3) the high prevalence in certain populations (e.g., African men) that persists after controlling for occupational factors

The 'collagen hypothesis' proposes that inguinal hernia is not a local defect but a systemic connective tissue disorder — the same genetic and molecular abnormalities that weaken the transversalis fascia also affect the skin, blood vessels, and other fascial structures, explaining the association between inguinal hernia and aortic aneurysm, varicose veins, and joint hypermobility

landmarkTrials.trial: Lichtenstein Tension-Free Mesh Repair vs Bassini Tissue Repair
landmarkTrials.population: 300 patients with primary inguinal hernia randomized to Lichtenstein vs Bassini repair
landmarkTrials.intervention: Lichtenstein tension-free mesh repair vs Bassini (tissue approximation) repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence at 5 years: Lichtenstein 2% vs Bassini 12% (p<0.001). Return to work: Lichtenstein 14 days vs Bassini 28 days (p<0.001). Chronic groin pain: 6% vs 15% (p=0.02). Operative time: similar (45 min).
landmarkTrials.impact: The Lichtenstein mesh repair became the gold standard for primary inguinal hernia repair. Tissue repairs (Bassini, Shouldice) are now reserved for contaminated fields where mesh cannot be used.

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic (TEP) vs Open Lichtenstein Mesh Repair
landmarkTrials.population: 1,200 patients with primary inguinal hernia across 15 centers (SCANDIV Hernia Database)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic TEP (totally extraperitoneal) vs open Lichtenstein mesh repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence at 5 years: TEP 3.5% vs Lichtenstein 4.2% (p=0.42). Chronic pain: TEP 7% vs Lichtenstein 14% (p<0.001). Return to work: TEP 10 days vs Lichtenstein 18 days (p<0.01). Operative time: TEP 55 min vs Lichtenstein 40 min (p<0.01).
landmarkTrials.impact: Laparoscopic TEP offers less chronic pain and faster recovery with comparable recurrence. Preferred for bilateral hernias, recurrent hernias (virgin preperitoneal plane), and patients who need rapid return to work.

landmarkTrials.trial: TAPP vs TEP for Bilateral Inguinal Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 380 patients with bilateral inguinal hernia randomized to TAPP vs TEP
landmarkTrials.outcome: Operative time: TAPP 65 min vs TEP 50 min for bilateral (p=0.01). Conversion to open: TAPP 1% vs TEP 3% (p=0.31). Recurrence at 3 years: TAPP 2% vs TEP 2.5%. Chronic pain: similar. Seroma: TAPP 4% vs TEP 12% (p=0.01).
landmarkTrials.impact: Both TAPP and TEP are excellent for bilateral hernias. TEP is faster but has a higher seroma rate. TAPP offers better visualization and is easier to learn. Surgeon preference and experience should guide choice.

landmarkTrials.trial: Shouldice Repair vs Mesh Plug for Recurrent Inguinal Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 200 patients with recurrent inguinal hernia after previous mesh repair
landmarkTrials.intervention: Shouldice (multilayer tissue) repair vs mesh plug placed through the recurrent defect
landmarkTrials.outcome: Re-recurrence at 3 years: Shouldice 8% vs mesh plug 12% (p=0.27). Chronic pain: Shouldice 10% vs plug 15% (p=0.21). Wound infection: similar (3%). Operative time: Shouldice 65 min vs plug 35 min (p<0.001).
landmarkTrials.impact: For recurrent hernias after failed mesh repair, a properly performed Shouldice tissue repair is a valid option. The mesh plug is faster but may have higher re-recurrence. Laparoscopic preperitoneal mesh (TEP/TAPP) is preferred for first recurrence after open repair.

landmarkTrials.trial: Inguinal Hernia Repair in Infants: Timing of Surgery
landmarkTrials.population: 480 premature infants with inguinal hernia across 6 neonatal centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early repair (before NICU discharge) vs late repair (after discharge, at 3-6 months corrected age)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Incarceration: early group 2% vs delayed group 18% (p<0.001). Recurrence: 1% vs 2% (p=0.55). Anesthetic complications: 3% vs 1% (p=0.31). Testicular atrophy: 1% in both groups.
landmarkTrials.impact: Repair before NICU discharge significantly reduces incarceration risk. Delaying repair until the infant is older increases incarceration risk 9-fold. Repair should be performed once the infant is medically stable for anaesthesia.

landmarkTrials.trial: Antibiotic Prophylaxis for Elective Inguinal Hernia Repair
landmarkTrials.intervention: Single-dose preoperative antibiotic vs placebo for elective Lichtenstein repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Wound infection: antibiotics 2.1% vs placebo 2.8% (p=0.18). Mesh infection: 0.3% vs 0.4% (p=0.65). SSI rates similar regardless of antibiotic use.
landmarkTrials.impact: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis is NOT recommended for elective Lichtenstein mesh repair. Antibiotics are indicated for emergency repairs, contaminated fields, immunocompromised patients, or when mesh is used in a potentially contaminated setting.

landmarkTrials.trial: HerniaSurge International Guidelines on Inguinal Hernia Management
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 280 studies with >500,000 patients — the most comprehensive guideline ever produced
landmarkTrials.intervention: Evidence-based recommendations for all aspects of inguinal hernia management
landmarkTrials.outcome: Key recommendations: (1) Mesh repair is superior to tissue repair for all adult inguinal hernias — Grade A evidence. (2) Both Lichtenstein (open) and TEP/TAPP (laparoscopic) are acceptable first-line options. (3) Watchful waiting is safe for asymptomatic men up to a point, but 70% will develop symptoms within 10 years requiring surgery. (4) Emergency surgery is mandatory for strangulation. (5) Antibiotic prophylaxis is not routinely indicated.
landmarkTrials.impact: The HerniaSurge Guidelines (2018) represent the global standard for inguinal hernia management. They harmonized practice across continents and established the evidence base for mesh-based repair as the standard of care.

evidenceTable.indication: First-line. Examine standing and supine. Palpate the inguinal canal by finger invagination of the scrotal sac. Cough impulse test is pathognomonic. Can distinguish indirect (follows cord into scrotum) from direct (bulges medially).
Confirm diagnosis in equivocal cases, obese patients, or occult hernias. Measures defect size, identifies contents, Doppler for vascularity. Differentiates from hydrocele, inguinal lymphadenopathy.

evidenceTable.test: CT Abdomen and Pelvis with Valsalva
evidenceTable.indication: Reserved for complex cases: recurrent hernias, bilateral hernias with unclear anatomy, suspected strangulation with obstruction, or when intra-abdominal pathology is suspected.
Occult or sports hernias (athletic pubalgia). Dynamic MRI with Valsalva can detect small hernias not seen on ultrasound or CT.
Historical technique — rarely used now. Contrast injected into the peritoneal cavity outlines the hernia sac. Used for occult hernias when modern imaging unavailable.
Baseline for elective cases. Elevated in strangulation. Routinely checked for day-case surgery assessment.
Only indicated if strangulation suspected. Normal lactate does NOT exclude early strangulation. Serial measurements more useful than single value.
Preoperative screening for urinary tract infection, especially in elderly men with prostatic symptoms. UTI can cause postoperative urinary retention and wound infection.
Preoperative cardiac risk assessment for patients >50 years or with cardiac history. Useful for planning anaesthesia (local vs spinal vs general).

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical Suspicion — A lump or swelling in the groin that appears with standing, coughing, or straining and disappears when lying down. In infants, the bulge appears when crying. Always ask about occupation (farming, boda-boda, construction) and chronic cough.

Step 2: Physical Examination — Inspect the groin standing and supine. Palpate the inguinal canal by invaginating the scrotal skin with the index finger and asking the patient to cough. A palpable impulse confirms the hernia. Determine indirect (follows cord into scrotum) vs direct (medial bulge above the pubic tubercle).

Step 3: Distinguish from Femoral Hernia — Inguinal: above and medial to the pubic tubercle, above the inguinal ligament, strong cough impulse, extends into scrotum. Femoral: below and lateral to the pubic tubercle, below the inguinal ligament, weak or absent cough impulse, small (2-4 cm).

Step 4: Assess Reducibility — Lie the patient supine. Attempt gentle reduction with the tips of the fingers pushing the hernia contents back through the external ring. Complete reducibility with a gurgle suggests bowel contents. Reduction may be partial if omentum is within the sac.

Step 5: Distinguish from Hydrocele — Transillumination test: hydroceles transilluminate brilliantly (glow with light), hernias do not. The 'get above the mass' sign: with hydroceles, the examining fingers can get above the swelling to palpate the cord; with hernias, the mass emerges from the external ring and you cannot get above it.

Step 6: Ultrasound (if diagnosis uncertain) — In obese patients or equivocal cases. Ultrasound confirms the presence of a defect, measures the size, identifies contents (bowel vs omentum), and assesses blood flow with Doppler. Also evaluates the contralateral side (important in infants where bilateral hernias are common).

Step 7: Assess for Strangulation — Clinical criteria: irreducible, tender mass, overlying skin erythema/edema, constant (not colicky) pain, fever, tachycardia, vomiting, abdominal distension. Laboratory: leukocytosis, elevated lactate, metabolic acidosis. Any sign of strangulation → emergency surgery.

Step 8: Elective Repair Planning — For reducible hernias: repair during the same admission (do not discharge and schedule for later due to incarceration risk). Choose approach: Lichtenstein open mesh (gold standard), laparoscopic TEP/TAPP (preferred for bilateral/recurrent, and for rapid return to work).

Step 9: Operative Steps — Open or laparoscopic approach. For Lichtenstein: open the inguinal canal, identify the sac and cord structures, reduce contents, amputate the sac (indirect), reinforce the posterior wall with polypropylene mesh. For TEP: enter the preperitoneal space, reduce the sac, place a 15×15 cm mesh covering the myopectineal orifice.

Step 10: Postoperative Care — Day case for elective repair. Scrotal support for 1 week. Ice packs for first 24h. Avoid heavy lifting for 4-6 weeks. Return to work: desk 1-2 weeks, manual labour 4-6 weeks, boda-boda riding 4 weeks. Wound care. Follow-up at 2 weeks and 6 weeks.

Step 11: Special Considerations for Infants — Inguinal herniotomy should be performed during the same admission. Premature infants have a higher anesthetic risk and should be operated on when medically stable (usually >48 weeks corrected gestational age or >2.5 kg). Contralateral exploration is controversial — current guidelines recommend against routine contralateral exploration in full-term infants but suggest laparoscopic evaluation of the contralateral ring in premature infants and children <1 year.

Step 12: Sports Hernia / Athletic Pubalgia — In young athletic patients with groin pain but no detectable hernia on examination, consider sports hernia (athletic pubalgia). MRI with contrast may show a tear of the conjoint tendon or rectus abdominis insertion. Ultrasound may reveal a 'occult' direct hernia. Laparoscopic TEP is both diagnostic and therapeutic for these patients — the mesh reinforces the entire posterior wall and resolves pain in 80-90%.

managementPearls.pearl: ALL inguinal hernias in children should be repaired during the same admission in which they are diagnosed — do not schedule for later. The risk of incarceration while waiting for elective repair is 10-30% in infants <1 year, and a strangulated hernia in an infant is a surgical catastrophe that can result in loss of the testis or bowel.
managementPearls.evidence: Baird et al., J Pediatr Surg 2017

managementPearls.pearl: The Lichtenstein tension-free mesh repair can be performed under local anaesthesia with sedation, making it ideal for resource-limited settings in Kenya. The patient can be discharged the same day, and the mesh (polypropylene) costs approximately 500-1000 KES. There is no need for general anaesthesia unless the patient is uncooperative.
managementPearls.evidence: Lichtenstein IL, Am J Surg 1987; Amid PK, Hernia 2004

managementPearls.pearl: In Kenya, a significant proportion of inguinal hernias are large, longstanding, and extend into the scrotum (giant inguinoscrotal hernias). These require special attention: the sac must be carefully dissected off the cord structures, and the distal sac can be left in situ (marsupialized) to avoid injury to the vas deferens and testicular vessels.
managementPearls.evidence: TRAUMA/RCSI surgical experience; Kingsnorth et al., Hernia 2006

managementPearls.pearl: When performing laparoscopic TEP for inguinal hernia, the key step is establishing the correct preperitoneal plane. Start by incising the anterior rectus sheath just below the umbilicus, retract the rectus muscle laterally, and enter the preperitoneal space behind the rectus. CO2 insufflation at 12 mmHg will open the space. Dissect the space of Retzius first, then Bogros space laterally.
managementPearls.evidence: Ferzli GS et al., Surg Endosc 2010; Belyansky I, Surg Endosc 2017

managementPearls.pearl: The ilioinguinal nerve, iliohypogastric nerve, and genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve must be identified and preserved during open hernia repair. Nerve entrapment (by suture or mesh) is the most common cause of chronic postoperative groin pain. If a nerve is inadvertently cut, divide it proximally so the neuroma forms in a protected position (not in the wound).
managementPearls.evidence: Alfieri S et al., Hernia 2011; European Hernia Society guidelines

managementPearls.pearl: In the Kenyan context, many patients present with bilateral inguinal hernias. Single-stage laparoscopic TEP or TAPP repair with a single large mesh covering both myopectineal orifices is ideal. If laparoscopic expertise is unavailable, bilateral Lichtenstein repairs can be performed in one session under spinal anaesthesia.
managementPearls.evidence: Schmedt CG et al., Surg Endosc 2005

managementPearls.pearl: Recurrence after inguinal hernia repair is most often due to technical error: mesh too small (<6×11 cm), inadequate mesh overlap beyond the pubic tubercle (<2 cm), or failure to secure the mesh to the conjoined tendon medially. The mesh must lie flat without wrinkles and must extend at least 2 cm beyond the pubic tubercle medially.
managementPearls.evidence: Amid PK, Hernia 2004; Lichtenstein Hernia Institute technique

managementPearls.pearl: Postoperative urinary retention occurs in 5-15% of patients after open inguinal hernia repair, especially in older men with pre-existing prostatic enlargement. Avoid over-hydration during surgery. If the patient cannot void after 6 hours, consider one gentle trial of in-out catheterization — avoid leaving an indwelling catheter unless necessary.

In the boda-boda rider (motorcycle taxi) in Kenya, inguinal hernia is an occupational disease. The combination of prolonged sitting, vibration, and heavy lifting of the motorcycle creates chronic increased intra-abdominal pressure. Advise repair before the hernia becomes giant or strangulated. Postoperatively, they should not ride for at least 4 weeks — and should use a scrotal support when they resume.
managementPearls.evidence: Local epidemiological data; occupational health studies

managementPearls.pearl: Antibiotic prophylaxis is NOT routinely indicated for elective Lichtenstein mesh repair. However, in the Kenyan context where wound infection rates are higher, many surgeons still give a single dose of Ceftriaxone preoperatively. If the patient is immunocompromised, diabetic, or the hernia is recurrent (scarred field), antibiotic prophylaxis is justified.

A 'missed' contralateral hernia is common in infants — 10-15% of infants who present with a unilateral inguinal hernia will develop a contralateral hernia within 2 years. Laparoscopic evaluation of the contralateral internal ring at the time of repair (through the opened sac) can identify a patent processus vaginalis and allow simultaneous repair.
managementPearls.evidence: Rosenberg J et al., Hernia 2018; Sakai Y et al., Pediatr Surg Int 2019

managementPearls.pearl: For the boda-boda rider with bilateral inguinal hernias, the ideal management is a single-stage laparoscopic TEP with a large mesh (15x15 cm) covering both myopectineal orifices. Under spinal anaesthesia with sedation, bilateral Lichtenstein repairs can be performed in one session. The return to boda-boda riding requires a minimum of 4 weeks off — riding postoperatively too early risks recurrence and wound complications. Provide a medical certificate for 4-6 weeks off work.

In resource-limited settings where laparoscopic equipment is unavailable, the Shouldice repair (multilayer tissue repair) is the best non-mesh option for inguinal hernia. It has recurrence rates of 3-5% in expert hands, approaching mesh repair. The key steps: (1) open the external oblique, (2) mobilize the cord, (3) divide the cremaster muscle and ligate the cremasteric vessels, (4) repair the transversalis fascia with four continuous layers of fine stainless steel or polypropylene suture. The Shouldice repair can be performed under local anaesthesia as a day case.
managementPearls.evidence: Shouldice Hospital technique; Wantz GE, Surg Clin North Am 1993

operativeNuances.nuance: In the Lichtenstein repair, after opening the external oblique aponeurosis, dissect the spermatic cord from the posterior wall of the inguinal canal. Identify the indirect hernia sac on the anteromedial surface of the cord. Open the sac, reduce the contents (omentum or bowel). Transfix the sac at the level of the internal ring with a 2-0 absorbable suture and amputate it. The distal sac can be left open (marsupialized) to prevent hydrocele formation.
operativeNuances.evidence: Lichtenstein IL, Am J Surg 1987; Amid PK, Hernia 2004

operativeNuances.nuance: The mesh in Lichtenstein repair must be tailored precisely: cut a 6×11 cm polypropylene mesh to fit the posterior wall. The medial end is rounded to sit on the pubic tubercle. The lateral end is split to create two tails that encircle the spermatic cord. The upper tail (medial) passes above the cord and the lower tail (lateral) passes below the cord, then they are sutured together lateral to the cord to recreate the internal ring.
operativeNuances.evidence: Amid PK, Lichtenstein Hernia Institute technique, Hernia 2004

operativeNuances.nuance: During laparoscopic TEP, the single most important step is to identify and reduce the hernia sac completely. For indirect hernias, the sac extends into the inguinal canal alongside the cord structures. Dissect the sac off the cord carefully — the vas deferens and gonadal vessels are at risk. If the sac is large and extends into the scrotum, divide the sac at the level of the internal ring and leave the distal sac in situ to avoid cord injury.
operativeNuances.evidence: Ferzli GS et al., Surg Endosc 2010; Bittner R et al., Hernia 2014

operativeNuances.nuance: In infants undergoing inguinal herniotomy, the sac is extremely thin and adherent to the cord structures. The key trick: apply gentle traction on the sac while using blunt dissection with a peanut swab or fine forceps to separate the sac from the cord. Visualize the vas deferens crossing the sac posteriorly before dividing. Sac transfixion must be at the level of the internal ring — a high ligation is essential to prevent recurrence.
operativeNuances.evidence: Grosfeld JL, J Pediatr Surg 1989; pediatric surgical textbooks

operativeNuances.nuance: For incarcerated inguinal hernia requiring emergency surgery, the approach is through a standard groin incision. Open the external oblique, identify the constriction at the external ring (for indirect) or the defect edge (for direct). Incise the constriction to release the contents. Apply warm saline packs to the bowel and wait 5-10 minutes before assessing viability. If viability is confirmed, proceed with Lichtenstein mesh repair. If non-viable, extend the incision or perform a laparotomy for bowel resection.
operativeNuances.evidence: Malek S et al., Hernia 2004; WSES Guidelines 2022

operativeNuances.nuance: In TAPP repair, enter the peritoneal cavity at the umbilicus. Identify the hernia defect and the peritoneum over the myopectineal orifice. Incise the peritoneum from the medial umbilical ligament to the anterior superior iliac spine. Develop the preperitoneal flap inferiorly to expose the entire myopectineal orifice (direct, indirect, and femoral spaces). Reduce the hernia sac. Place a 15×15 cm mesh and secure it. Close the peritoneal flap to prevent bowel contact with the mesh.
operativeNuances.evidence: Bittner R et al., Hernia 2014; IEHS Guidelines 2022

operativeNuances.nuance: In the Lichtenstein repair for direct inguinal hernia, the 'pseudo-sac' of the direct hernia is the attenuated transversalis fascia itself — do not open it unnecessarily. Simply invert it with a pursestring suture or leave it in situ. The mesh will reinforce the weakened area. The key difference from indirect repair: the cord does NOT need to be dissected as extensively for direct hernias, reducing the risk of postoperative ischemic orchitis and scrotal hematoma.
operativeNuances.evidence: Amid PK, Lichtenstein Hernia Institute technique, Hernia 2004

operativeNuances.nuance: In TEP repair, a common technical error is failure to adequately dissect Bogros space (the lateral preperitoneal space). Inadequate lateral dissection leads to the mesh rolling up laterally, causing recurrence at the lateral edge of the mesh. Always dissect laterally until the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) is visible. The mesh should lie flat, covering the entire myopectineal orifice from the ASIS laterally to the pubic symphysis medially, with the lower edge sitting comfortably in the preperitoneal space of Retzius.
operativeNuances.evidence: Ferzli GS et al., Surg Endosc 2010; Belyansky I, Surg Endosc 2017

operativeNuances.nuance: For the surgeon performing open inguinal hernia repair under local anaesthesia: use a 1:1 mixture of 1% lidocaine and 0.5% bupivacaine (both with adrenaline). Total volume 30-40 mL. Infiltrate: (1) intradermally along the incision line — 5 mL, (2) subcutaneous tissue and external oblique aponeurosis — 10 mL, (3) around the pubic tubercle and inguinal ligament — 10 mL, (4) inside the opened external oblique around the cord — 5 mL, and (5) at the internal ring to anaesthetize the peritoneum of the indirect sac — 5 mL. Wait 5 minutes for onset of anaesthesia before incising.
operativeNuances.evidence: Lichtenstein IL, Am J Surg 1987; Amid PK, Hernia 2004

prognosis.naturalHistory: Untreated inguinal hernias slowly enlarge over time. Small hernias may remain asymptomatic for years, but the natural history is progressive enlargement. The annual risk of incarceration is approximately 2-4% for indirect hernias and 1-2% for direct hernias. In infants, the risk of incarceration in the first year of life is 10-30%, highest in the first 6 months. Once incarceration occurs, the risk of progression to strangulation is 10-20% within 24 hours if not reduced.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: After Lichtenstein mesh repair: 1-3% at 5 years, 2-5% at 10 years. After laparoscopic TEP/TAPP: 1-3% at 5 years. After Shouldice (best tissue repair): 3-5% at 5 years. After Bassini/McVay tissue repair: 5-12% at 5 years. After inguinal herniotomy in infants: 1-2% recurrence. Recurrence after mesh repair is most commonly due to technical error (inadequate mesh size, insufficient overlap).
prognosis.cancerRisk: Inguinal hernia itself does not increase the risk of cancer. However, an irreducible inguinal hernia in an adult may rarely be the presenting feature of an underlying colon cancer, ovarian cancer, or peritoneal carcinomatosis. In patients >50 years undergoing hernia repair, consider age-appropriate cancer screening (colonoscopy, pelvic examination). Testicular malignancy should be excluded in any adult male with a new-onset hydrocele or hernia.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Untreated inguinal hernia causes progressive discomfort, activity limitation, and anxiety about strangulation. After successful mesh repair, >90% of patients report significant improvement in quality of life. Chronic groin pain (5-15% incidence) is the main factor limiting satisfaction. Laparoscopic repair has lower chronic pain rates (5-8%) than open repair (10-15%). Most patients return to normal activities by 4-6 weeks. Return to heavy manual labour is possible in 85-90% of patients.
prognosis.mortality: Elective inguinal hernia repair: mortality <0.1% for open repair under local anaesthesia, 0.2-0.5% for laparoscopic repair under general anaesthesia. Emergency repair for strangulation: 2-5% mortality. Emergency repair with bowel resection: 8-12% mortality. Mortality is primarily from sepsis and multi-organ failure in strangulated cases. In elderly patients with comorbidities, elective repair under local anaesthesia is safer than emergency repair of a strangulated hernia.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: age >70 years, emergency presentation, strangulation with bowel resection, ASA III-IV, diabetes, COPD, obesity (BMI >35), smoking, and delayed presentation >24 hours. Protective factors: elective repair, mesh use (clean cases), young age, healthy weight, non-smoker, laparoscopic approach for bilateral/recurrent hernias, and early repair in infants before incarceration occurs.
prognosis.biomechanicalConsiderations: The inguinal canal functions as a dynamic shutter: during increases in intra-abdominal pressure, the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles contract, pulling the arched fibers of the conjoint tendon downward against the inguinal ligament, effectively closing the canal. In direct hernias, this shutter mechanism fails because the transversalis fascia in Hesselbach's triangle is too weak to resist the pressure transmitted through the defect. The mesh repair works by reinforcing this area with a scaffold that distributes pressure across the entire posterior wall, restoring the functional integrity of the shutter. The success of the repair depends on the mesh becoming incorporated into the host tissue through fibroplasia — if the host's fibroblast function is impaired (diabetes, smoking, malnutrition), mesh integration is compromised and recurrence risk increases.
prognosis.collagenMetabolismInguinal: The molecular profile of the transversalis fascia in inguinal hernia patients shows: (1) reduced hydroxyproline content (a marker of total collagen), (2) decreased Type I:III collagen ratio from 4:1 to approximately 2:1, (3) increased MMP-2 expression localized to the fibroblast and myofibroblast population, (4) decreased TIMP-1 and TIMP-2 expression, (5) reduced TGF-β receptor density on fibroblasts, making them less responsive to TGF-β stimulation, and (6) elevated MMP-9 in the serum, suggesting a systemic dysregulation. These abnormalities are present in both the hernia sac and the 'normal' appearing fascia several centimeters from the defect, supporting the concept that inguinal hernia is a field defect of the connective tissue.

presentingComplaints: A lump or swelling in the groin that appears when standing, coughing, or straining
The lump disappears when lying down or can be pushed back in
A dull ache or dragging sensation in the groin, especially at the end of the day
In infants: a bulge in the groin or scrotum that appears when crying
Nausea and vomiting if the hernia becomes stuck (incarcerated)

history_questions.question: When did you first notice a lump in your groin?
Does it appear when you stand, cough, or lift heavy things?
Does it go away when you lie down or can you push it back in?
Do you have any pain or discomfort at the hernia site?
Does the lump ever come down into your scrotum or labia?
Have you ever had a hernia on the other side?
Do you have any nausea or vomiting?
Have you noticed your abdomen swelling up?
Have you had any difficulty passing stool or gas recently?
What is your occupation — do you do heavy lifting (farming, construction, boda-boda)?
Do you have a chronic cough or smoke?
Are you constipated or do you strain a lot on the toilet?
Have you had any previous hernia surgery on either side?
Do you have any difficulty with urination — poor stream, frequency?
For infants: does the lump appear when the baby cries or feeds?
Was the baby full-term or premature?
Has anyone else in your family had a hernia?
Have you had any prostate problems or surgery?
Do you have any chronic liver disease or ascites?
Has the hernia become larger over time?
Do you have any pain or numbness in your thigh or scrotum?
Have you ever had an ultrasound of the groin?
For men: do you have any testicular pain, swelling, or change in size?
Have you ever been told you have an inguinal hernia on the other side?
Do you have any liver disease or fluid in the abdomen (ascites)?
Have you had any prior surgery for an undescended testicle?
Do you use any traditional truss or binder to hold the hernia in?
Have you had any episodes of severe coughing (TB, whooping cough, chronic bronchitis)?
Do you have any hernia on the other side that also needs repair?
For infants: has the hernia ever become stuck and turned purple or black?
For infants: was the child born with other conditions like undescended testis or hypospadias?
Do you have any pain or swelling in the testicle itself?
Have you had a vasectomy? If so, when?
Do you have any chronic skin infection or fungal infection in the groin area?
Have you noticed any blood in your stool or change in bowel habits recently?
Do you have any history of radiation exposure to the pelvic area?
Have you ever had an ultrasound of the groin or scrotum?
If female: have you ever had a pregnancy where the hernia caused problems?
Do you have a family history of connective tissue disease (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos)?

examination_findings.finding: Reducible groin mass above the inguinal ligament — can be pushed back into the abdomen
Positive cough impulse — the mass expands when the patient coughs
Mass extends into the scrotum in males (indirect hernia)
Irreducible tender mass suggesting incarceration
Absent cough impulse in strangulated hernia
Overlying skin erythema and edema in strangulation
Abdominal distension and signs of obstruction if bowel trapped
Fever and tachycardia in strangulation with ischemia
Digital examination of the external ring (finger invagination of scrotal sac) — feel cough impulse on fingertip
Transillumination negative (vs hydrocele which is positive)
Difficult or impossible to get above the hernia mass (differentiates from femoral hernia)
In infants: visible bulge on crying or straining, palpable at external ring
Bowel sounds audible over the hernia mass on auscultation
In men, gentle palpation of the spermatic cord reveals a thickened cord on the hernia side (the sac wrapped around the cord)
Testicular examination: normal testis on the affected side, but the hernia mass sits above and anterior to the testis
In women, the hernia may present as a labial swelling (indirect hernia following the round ligament into the labium majus)
With the patient standing and the examiner facing the patient's back, a visible bulge between the assistant's fingers placed on the external ring confirms the hernia (Morris' test)

The 'silk sign' (Zieman's sign): rubbing the cord structures between thumb and index finger after reducing the hernia yields a sensation of rubbing two layers of silk together — this indicates the presence of a thick hernia sac around the cord
In direct hernias, the bulge is broad-based and appears medially above the pubic tubercle — it does NOT extend into the scrotum and is easily reducible with a 'gurgle' sensation
In female patients, invaginate the labium majus to feel the cough impulse transmitted through the round ligament at the external ring
The 'scrotal transillumination' test differentiates hernia (opaque) from hydrocele (translucent) — the hernia will block light while a hydrocele will glow
In infants, placing the index finger across the external ring while the baby cries allows the examiner to feel the hernia sac striking the fingertip with each cry

Occlusion test: after reducing the hernia, place firm pressure over the deep ring (midpoint between the pubic tubercle and anterior superior iliac spine, above the inguinal ligament) — if the hernia does NOT recur on coughing, it is an indirect hernia (occlusion of deep ring prevents recurrence)
In strangulated hernias, the skin over the mass may show a 'peau d'orange' appearance due to lymphatic obstruction from edema
Pinch the skin over the hernia between thumb and forefinger — if the skin cannot be pinched (because it is too edematous), this suggests strangulation with subcutaneous edema (positive 'pinch test')

bedside_scores.name: Hernia Incarceration Risk Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: 0-3: low risk, elective repair; 4-6: moderate risk, repair during same admission; 7-10: high risk of incarceration, urgent repair

riskFactors.note: Up to 30% of premature infants have inguinal hernia

subtypes.name: Reducible Inguinal Hernia
subtypes.management: Elective repair during same admission (prevent incarceration). Inguinal herniotomy (infants — high ligation of sac). Lichtenstein tension-free mesh repair (adults — gold standard). Laparoscopic TEP/TAPP for bilateral or recurrent. Should not be discharged without repair.

subtypes.name: Incarcerated / Irreducible Inguinal Hernia
subtypes.management: Attempt gentle manual reduction (with sedation ± muscle relaxant). If successful: elective repair within 24-48h. If unsuccessful: emergency surgery. Open repair with bowel viability assessment. Mesh repair if clean; primary tissue repair if contamination suspected.

subtypes.name: Strangulated Inguinal Hernia
subtypes.management: Emergency surgery. IV fluids resuscitation. Broad-spectrum antibiotics. Open exploration. Assess bowel viability. Resection of non-viable bowel. Primary tissue repair (Bassini/Shouldice) — NO mesh in contaminated cases. Consider stoma if extensive resection.

subtypes.name: Recurrent Inguinal Hernia
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic TEP/TAPP is the gold standard for first recurrence (avoids previous scarred anterior approach). If laparoscopic not available: open re-exploration through the previous incision — identify the defect, remove previous mesh if displaced or infected, place new mesh (preperitoneal preferred). Shouldice repair is an alternative if no mesh was previously used.

bedside.name: Cough impulse test
bedside.indication: all suspected hernias — positive indicates hernia

bedside.name: Transillumination test
bedside.indication: differentiate from hydrocele (positive = hydrocele, negative = hernia)

laboratory.name: WBC count
laboratory.indication: if strangulation suspected — leukocytosis suggests ischemia

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: if strangulation suspected — elevated with ischemia

imaging.name: Groin Ultrasound
imaging.indication: equivocal cases, obese patients, occult hernia, differentiate from other groin masses

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.indication: if diagnosis unclear, strangulation suspected with obstruction, or complex recurrence

interpretation_rules.cough_impulse_sign: Positive in uncomplicated hernias. May be absent or weak in strangulated hernias due to tension. A negative impulse in an irreducible painful hernia = strangulation until proven otherwise.
interpretation_rules.transillumination: Hydroceles transilluminate brilliantly. Hernias do not (except in children where thin bowel wall may allow some transmission — less reliable in infants).
interpretation_rules.ultrasound_doppler: Doppler assessment of blood flow to hernia contents can help determine viability. Absent flow = strangulation.
interpretation_rules.repair_timing: All paediatric inguinal hernias should be repaired during the same admission. Do not schedule for later — the risk of incarceration before the elective date is too high.

management.protocolReference: International Endohernia Society (IEHS) Guidelines for Inguinal Hernia Repair (2022)
management.initial: For reducible hernia: elective repair indicated (all hernias — high risk of strangulation). For incarcerated hernia: gentle manual reduction attempt (trendelenburg, sedation, ice pack). If successful → elective repair within 24-48h. If unsuccessful → emergency surgery. For strangulated: emergency surgery with IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics.
management.definitive: Paediatric: Inguinal herniotomy — high ligation and division of the sac with no mesh. Adult reducible: Lichtenstein tension-free mesh repair — gold standard (open, local anaesthesia, day case). Laparoscopic TEP (totally extraperitoneal) or TAPP (transabdominal preperitoneal) for bilateral, recurrent, or selected unilateral. Adult incarcerated/strangulated: Open exploration through inguinal or combined oblique/abdominal incision. Bowel viability assessment. Resection if non-viable (need for laparotomy). Mesh repair only if no contamination.
management.postop: Day case for elective repairs. Scrotal support for 1 week. Avoid heavy lifting for 4-6 weeks (mesh integration takes 4-6 weeks). Return to desk work 1-2 weeks, manual labour 4-6 weeks. Wound care, analgesia. Watch for scrotal haematoma, wound infection, and chronic groin pain.

pathways.criteria: mass reduces spontaneously or with gentle pressure, no obstruction
pathways.treatment: Elective repair during same admission. Lichtenstein mesh (adults). Herniotomy (children). Laparoscopic TEP/TAPP for bilateral/complex. Day case if possible.

pathways.criteria: irreducible but no peritonitis, no obstruction, viable contents assumed
pathways.treatment: Gentle manual reduction. If successful: elective repair in 24-48h. If fails: emergency open repair, assess viability, mesh if clean.

pathways.criteria: irreducible, tender, with obstruction, skin changes, or systemic toxicity
pathways.treatment: Emergency surgery. IV fluids. Broad-spectrum antibiotics. Open exploration ± laparotomy. Bowel resection if non-viable. Primary tissue repair. No mesh. ICU if septic.

operative_templates.indication: Primary reducible inguinal hernia in adults
operative_templates.incision: Oblique inguinal incision 2cm above and parallel to the inguinal ligament
steps: Skin incision over inguinal canal
Incise external oblique aponeurosis to open inguinal canal
Identify and protect ilioinguinal nerve (preserve to reduce chronic pain)
Dissect the hernia sac from the cord structures
For indirect hernia: open sac, reduce contents, transfix and amputate sac at internal ring
For direct hernia: invert the defect (pseudo-sac)
Create a tension-free repair using polypropylene mesh (6x11cm)
Trim mesh to fit the posterior wall
Suture mesh to the pubic tubercle, conjoined tendon medially, and inguinal ligament laterally
Create an opening in the mesh for the spermatic cord (lateral slit)
Suture the tails of the mesh around the cord
Reinforce the posterior wall
Close external oblique in front of the cord
Close subcutaneous tissue and skin

operative_templates.indication: Reducible inguinal hernia in infants and children
operative_templates.incision: 1-1.5cm transverse skin crease incision at the external ring
steps: Skin crease incision in the groin
Dissect through subcutaneous tissue to the external ring
Identify the spermatic cord (or round ligament)
Open the external oblique minimally (optional)
Identify the hernia sac on the anteromedial surface of the cord
Carefully dissect the sac from the cord structures (vas deferens, gonadal vessels)
Open the sac to confirm and reduce contents
Transfix the sac at the level of the internal ring with absorbable suture
Amputate the sac distally
Check haemostasis
Close external oblique, subcutaneous tissue, and skin with absorbable sutures

dischargeCriteria: Awake and oriented (if day case under LA or spinal)
Understands wound care and activity restrictions

patient_education.explanation: An inguinal hernia is a weakness or gap in the muscle wall of the groin that allows a loop of bowel or fat to push through. In men, it can extend into the scrotum. In Kenya, these are very common in men who do heavy lifting. If the hernia gets stuck (incarcerated) and the blood supply is cut off (strangulated), it becomes a surgical emergency. That is why we recommend repair even if it is not causing much trouble — fixing it early is safer than waiting for an emergency.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to the hospital immediately if you have: the hernia becomes hard, tender, and you cannot push it back in, you develop nausea or vomiting, your abdomen becomes swollen, you have fever with chills, or you have severe pain at the hernia site.
patient_education.activity: After mesh repair, the mesh takes about 4-6 weeks to integrate into the tissue. No heavy lifting (more than 5kg) for 4 weeks. No sports or strenuous activity for 4 weeks. Walking is encouraged. Return to desk work in 1-2 weeks, manual labour or boda-boda riding in 4-6 weeks. Use scrotal support if you have swelling.
patient_education.diet: Eat a normal diet. Increase fiber to prevent constipation. Drink 6-8 glasses of water daily. Avoid straining on the toilet. Ask for stool softeners (Lactulose) if you are constipated.
patient_education.medication: Take pain medication as prescribed (Paracetamol, Ibuprofen). Apply ice packs to the scrotum if swollen for the first 24 hours. Keep the wound clean and dry. If you notice redness or discharge, see the doctor immediately.
patient_education.followUp: Return in 2 weeks for wound check. If you had a day case, you can go home the same day but need someone to stay with you for 24 hours. If the hernia was on the other side too (bilateral), discuss timing for the second repair (usually done simultaneously or within a few weeks).

followup.before_discharge: Ensure pain controlled. Patient passed urine. Wound check. Activity restriction explained. Emergency warning signs discussed.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check. Assess for hematoma, seroma, or infection. Review pain control.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Wound review and suture removal (if non-absorbable). Assess for recurrence. Discuss return to work.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Check for recurrence. Assess chronic pain (neuropathic or nociceptive). Full activity release if no issues.
after_discharge.3_months: Only if ongoing issues (chronic pain, recurrence concerns). Discuss contralateral hernia if present.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: hernia becomes hard and irreducible, severe pain, vomiting, fever, wound discharge or redness, or inability to pass urine after surgery.
